fragments.content: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joints and tissues due to chronic hyperuricemia (serum urate >6.8 mg/dL - saturation point). Hyperuricemia results from decreased renal excretion (90% of cases) or increased production. Risk factors: male sex, obesity, alcohol (especially beer), high-purine diet (red meat, seafood), fructose-sweetened beverages, medications (thiazides, loop diuretics, niacin, cyclosporine, tacrolimus), CKD, metabolic syndrome, genetic variants (URAT1, GLUT9). Clinical phases: asymptomatic hyperuricemia; acute gouty arthritis (sudden severe pain, swelling, erythema - classically first MTP joint 'podagra'); intercritical gout (asymptomatic between flares); chronic tophaceous gout (urate deposits in joints, bones, soft tissues, ears). Tophi pathognomonic but late finding. Gout associated with increased cardiovascular and renal disease risk.

Acute flare treatment (start within 24 hours): NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen, celecoxib); colchicine 1.2mg then 0.6mg 1 hour later, then 0.6mg daily-bid (lower doses than historical); glucocorticoids (prednisone 30-40mg daily × 5-10 days, or intra-articular). IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra) for refractory cases. Urate-lowering therapy (ULT) indications: ≥2 flares/year, tophi, CKD stage ≥3, urolithiasis, serum urate >9mg/dL. First-line: allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) start 100mg/day (50mg if CKD), titrate every 2-5 weeks to target urate <6mg/dL (<5mg/dL if tophaceous); max 800mg/day. Febuxostat alternative (cardiovascular safety concerns). Probenecid (uricosuric) if eGFR >50. Pegloticase (recombinant uricase) for refractory severe gout. Flare prophylaxis when starting ULT: colchicine 0.6mg daily-bid, low-dose NSAID, or prednisone ≤10mg/day for ≥6 months. Lifestyle: weight loss, limit alcohol/purines, avoid fructose beverages.

Gold standard: synovial fluid analysis showing needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarized light microscopy. ACR/EULAR classification criteria (2015) use scoring system: clinical pattern (joint involvement, flare characteristics), time course, clinical signs (erythema, tophi), serum urate level, imaging findings. Serum urate often normal during acute flare (urate shifts into joints) - recheck after flare resolves. Imaging: X-ray shows punched-out erosions with overhanging edges in chronic gout; ultrasound shows 'double contour sign' (urate on cartilage surface), tophi; dual-energy CT specifically identifies urate deposits. Differential diagnosis: septic arthritis (always consider, especially monoarticular), pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate crystals - positively birefringent, rhomboid), cellulitis, trauma. Evaluate for comorbidities: renal function, cardiovascular risk factors, metabolic syndrome components.
